topic: Verdict Assignment Criteria — new section in Evaluation Guide (EXP-087)
our_argument_summary: The section defines the six verdict categories, establishes a 'primary-issue rule' for WINs with multiple failure modes, explicitly distinguishes Misleading from Std Model Explains, enumerates Misleading subtypes without formally splitting the category, explains Self-Contradicted as needing no external physics, and transparently acknowledges the absence of a 'Confirmed' category with stated criteria for adding one. Written in response to the holistic taxonomy review (HOL-TAXONOMY) which found the Misleading/Std Model Explains boundary unstated and the category overloaded.

holes_found.description: False-dilemma framing listed as Misleading subtype is inconsistent with WIN-054 reclassification. The section lists 'false-dilemma framing (citing a competitor's unsolved problem as dome evidence without offering dome-specific physics)' as one of four Misleading subtypes. But WIN-054 (El Gordo) was reclassified from Misleading to Not Demonstrated for exactly this pattern — the holistic review argued that citing a genuine anomaly isn't 'misleading,' it's 'not demonstrated.' Several remaining Misleading WINs (WIN-047, WIN-048, WIN-052, WIN-055) exhibit the same false-dilemma pattern. A dome defender reading these criteria will note the contradiction: if false-dilemma framing is a Misleading subtype, why was WIN-054 moved out? If WIN-054 was moved because pure false-dilemma is Not Demonstrated, why are WIN-047/048/052/055 still Misleading? The answer is likely that the remaining WINs have additional mishandling beyond the false-dilemma pattern, but the section doesn't state this distinction.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) qualify the false-dilemma subtype: 'false-dilemma framing combined with other mishandling (e.g., citing eroded significance as current, misattributing mechanisms)' to distinguish from pure false-dilemma cases that belong in Not Demonstrated, or (b) remove false-dilemma from the Misleading subtype list and note that pure false-dilemma is Not Demonstrated. Option (a) is simpler and preserves the current classifications.

holes_found.description: Asymmetric count-inflation standard not addressed. The section rightly lists 'count inflation (the same observation counted as multiple WINs)' as a Misleading subtype. But our own review counts five tidal WINs (045/046/049/050/051) as separate Self-Contradicted verdicts when all five derive from one geometric argument: the dome's local moon cannot produce semidiurnal tides. A dome defender reading the Verdict Assignment Criteria will immediately ask: 'You criticize us for counting M2/S2/K1/O1/N2 as five wins, but you count them as five self-contradictions. Isn't that the same thing?' The EXP-087 proposal itself noted this in 'also_recommends' but the integrated text doesn't acknowledge it.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief note, either here or in the Self-Contradicted paragraph: 'Note: five tidal WINs (045–051) share a single underlying geometric contradiction. We assign each its own verdict because each is a distinct claim in the dome's count, but we acknowledge these five derive from one root cause — a transparency standard we also apply to the dome's counting methodology.' This preempts the hypocrisy objection.

holes_found.description: The 'actively mishandle' language in the Misleading/Std Model Explains boundary implies intent. The key distinguishing question is phrased as: 'does the dome actively mishandle the evidence, or does it simply present a real observation that standard physics already explains?' The word 'actively' implies deliberate intent, while the Misleading definition itself uses passive voice ('the evidence has been mishandled'). A dome defender could argue: 'Our mistakes were honest errors, not active mishandling. By your own criteria, honest mistakes aren't Misleading.' The structural argument (evidence HAS BEEN mishandled, regardless of intent) is stronger and doesn't open this line of attack.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'does the dome actively mishandle the evidence' to 'has the evidence been mishandled in the dome's presentation' or 'does the dome's presentation structurally mishandle the evidence.' Remove the implication of intent — the verdict should classify the quality of the argument, not the character of the arguer.

holes_found.description: Primary-issue rule example covers only Refuted vs Std Model but not the arguably harder boundary cases. The worked example shows: wrong numbers AND standard physics → pick Refuted if numbers are verifiably wrong, pick Std Model if numbers are correct but non-discriminating. Good. But what about: (a) Self-Contradicted AND Refuted — if the dome's own equations contradict itself AND external data also contradicts it, which takes priority? (b) Misleading AND Self-Contradicted — if the dome misrepresents data AND the corrected data still contradicts dome geometry? These are not hypothetical — WIN-012 has been flagged for verdict-mismatch between Self-Contradicted and Not Demonstrated. A second worked example covering a Self-Contradicted boundary case would strengthen the rule.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a second example: 'If a WIN's dome-derived formula contradicts its own claimed value (Self-Contradicted) AND the data is also misrepresented (Misleading), the primary issue is the self-contradiction — because even with correct data the dome's own math fails. Self-Contradicted takes priority when internal consistency alone is sufficient to invalidate the claim.'

holes_found.description: TLDR uses 'most structurally damaging failure takes priority' while the body text uses 'the one that, if corrected, would still leave the claim unsupported.' These are subtly different criteria. 'Most structurally damaging' implies severity ranking (which the next TLDR sentence says doesn't exist), while 'if corrected, still unsupported' is a counterfactual test. The body text's formulation is superior. The TLDR is not wrong — 'structurally damaging' can refer to which failure is most fundamental within a specific WIN — but the phrasing creates a tension with 'not ranked by severity' in the same sentence.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider revising the TLDR to: 'When a WIN fails in multiple ways, we assign the verdict matching the failure that would persist even if the other issues were corrected.' This is more precise and avoids the apparent tension with the severity disclaimer. Minor — the current TLDR is defensible but slightly confusing.

holes_found.description: Hardcoded 'six' count appears four times ('The six verdict categories,' 'six categories,' etc.). The project's anti-hardcoding rule ('Never hardcode a number that can be derived from the data') nominally applies here, though the verdict category count is architectural rather than data-derived and unlikely to change without a major restructure. Still, the section itself discusses adding a 'Confirmed' category, which would make it seven.
holes_found.recommendation: Low priority. If a seventh category is ever added, these four instances need updating. Consider computing from the verdict-legend div count if the build system supports it, or accept the hardcode as architectural. Not worth a patch cycle.

stronger_arguments.description: The 'no Confirmed category' paragraph could be strengthened by explicitly addressing the dome defender's strongest counterargument: 'Any observation can be attributed to standard physics because standard physics is so broad — your criteria are unfalsifiable.' The section's criteria require that standard physics 'could not explain' the observation, but standard physics is a vast framework that can usually offer some explanation. The rebuttal is straightforward: we don't require that standard physics has zero explanatory power, only that the dome prediction be quantitatively distinguishable — that the dome predicts a DIFFERENT NUMBER than standard physics for the same observable. Currently 0 of 70 WINs achieve this. But the distinction between 'standard physics cannot explain' and 'dome predicts a different number' should be stated.
sources: Falsifiability section (eg-falsifiability) Condition 1 already makes this distinction well — the eg-verdicts section should echo it

stronger_arguments.description: The section would benefit from a brief statement about how verdicts can change. The text currently reads as static taxonomy, but the review has already reclassified WINs (e.g., WIN-054 Misleading→Not Demonstrated, WIN-053 to Self-Contradicted). A sentence acknowledging that verdicts are updated when evidence or analysis improves would demonstrate intellectual honesty and preempt the 'they keep changing their answers' objection.
sources: HOL-TAXONOMY deeper_analysis: 'each change should be accompanied by a clear rationale'

kernel_of_truth.description: This section fills a genuine and important gap. The holistic taxonomy review correctly identified that unstated decision criteria — particularly at the Misleading/Std Model Explains boundary — were the review's most exploitable process vulnerability. The six categories ARE conceptually sound; the problem was always in the application, not the definitions. Making the assignment criteria explicit, auditable, and AI-readable is exactly the right response. The primary-issue rule is a real contribution: it converts an implicit vibes-based sorting into a stated counterfactual test that can be challenged on its merits rather than dismissed as arbitrary.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The section addresses the taxonomy gap well but creates a new vulnerability at the false-dilemma subtype by enshrining a classification that contradicts the WIN-054 precedent. The section also doesn't address the most damaging meta-objection: that the review's own counting methodology (five tidal WINs = five Self-Contradicted) mirrors the very count inflation it criticizes in the dome.

tldr_review.tldr_text: Each WIN receives the verdict matching its primary failure mode. When a WIN fails in multiple ways, the most structurally damaging failure takes priority. The six categories are not ranked by severity — they describe different kinds of failure, not degrees of it.
tldr_review.notes: Minor tension between 'most structurally damaging takes priority' and 'not ranked by severity' — see holes_found. Factually correct but could be clearer.

cross_references_checked.reference: Part 3 link in Misleading subtypes paragraph

cross_references_checked.status: consistent — both use structural mishandling framing, verdict-legend now includes 'same observation counted as multiple independent wins'
addressed — boundary criterion now explicit, Misleading subtypes enumerated with rationale for not splitting
INCONSISTENCY — false-dilemma subtype still listed under Misleading despite WIN-054 moving out for that reason. See major hole.

deeper_analysis:
The Verdict Assignment Criteria section is the most important methodological addition since the restructure. It transforms implicit editorial judgment into stated, auditable rules. The primary-issue rule is well-formulated and the Misleading/Std Model Explains boundary — previously the most exploitable ambiguity — now has a clear decision criterion. The section correctly avoids the trap of splitting Misleading into formal subcategories (which would create more boundary disputes than it resolves) while transparently acknowledging the internal diversity.

The main weakness is at the intersection of the false-dilemma subtype and the WIN-054 precedent. The section was written by the analyst responding to curmudgeon feedback; the WIN-054 reclassification was a separate decider action also responding to curmudgeon feedback. These two responses are now slightly inconsistent. The fix is simple: qualify 'false-dilemma framing' as a compound failure (false-dilemma + additional mishandling) rather than a standalone Misleading subtype.

The count-inflation asymmetry (five tidal Self-Contradicted WINs from one argument) is the most dangerous unaddressed objection. The section talks about count inflation under Misleading but doesn't acknowledge that our own verdict assignments exhibit the same pattern. This isn't fatal — each tidal WIN IS individually self-contradicted — but failing to acknowledge the parallel gives a dome defender a cheap rhetorical win. A one-sentence preemption would neutralize this.

From the other side: a dome defender reading this section will appreciate the transparency (stated criteria, auditable rules) but will attack on three fronts: (1) the primary-issue rule assumes standard physics as baseline, (2) the 'no Confirmed category' criteria are unfalsifiably strict, and (3) the count-inflation criticism is applied asymmetrically. Fronts 1 and 2 are philosophically interesting but empirically addressable (the dome hasn't produced discriminating predictions regardless of framework). Front 3 is a process objection we should preempt.

citation_check.notes: No external citations in this section — all references are internal (Part 3 link, verdict-legend definitions). Internal cross-references verified.
